stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] The nurse is receiving the 07:00 shift report on a client admitted with a hemorrhagic stroke. Based on the provided clinical data, the nurse should identify the priority actions, the potential condition, and the parameters to monitor by dragging the choices to the bowtie.

draggableOptions.text: Elevate head of bed to 30 degrees
draggableOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. Elevating the head of the bed promotes venous drainage from the cranium via gravity. This intervention helps lower cerebral blood volume and intracranial pressure, which is a critical non-pharmacological treatment.

Correct. Mannitol is an osmotic diuretic that increases serum osmolality, drawing edematous fluid from the brain's interstitial space into the vasculature. This directly reduces cerebral edema and lowers intracranial pressure.

Incorrect. Administering hypotonic fluids would cause fluid to shift from the intravascular space into the brain cells, exacerbating cerebral edema. This action is contraindicated and would dangerously increase intracranial pressure.

draggableOptions.text: Place client in a supine position
draggableOptions.hoverRationale: Incorrect. Placing the client supine or in a Trendelenburg position impedes venous outflow from the brain. This would acutely raise intracranial pressure and significantly increase the risk of brain herniation.

Correct. The client's clinical picture, including Cushing's triad, pupillary changes, and CT findings of a large hemorrhage with midline shift, indicates severely increased intracranial pressure. This is the primary life-threatening condition.

Incorrect. SIADH involves fluid retention and dilutional hyponatremia. This client has hypernatremia and high serum osmolality, which are the opposite findings and are likely a result of osmotic diuresis from mannitol.

Incorrect. Hypovolemic shock presents with hypotension and tachycardia. This client is severely hypertensive and bradycardic, which are classic components of Cushing's triad, a physiological response to increased intracranial pressure.

draggableOptions.text: Continuous ICP monitoring via external ventricular drain (EVD)
draggableOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. Continuous ICP monitoring via an EVD provides real-time intracranial pressure data, which is essential for titrating therapies to prevent herniation. The EVD also allows for therapeutic drainage of cerebrospinal fluid.

Correct. Monitoring serum osmolality is critical when using osmotic diuretics like mannitol. It ensures therapeutic effectiveness while preventing excessive dehydration and potential acute kidney injury from over-diuresis.

Incorrect. While daily weights monitor overall fluid balance, they are not a sensitive or immediate indicator for guiding acute osmotic therapy. Serum osmolality provides a more direct and timely measure of the treatment's effect.

content.note: Client is a 72-year-old male brought to the ED by ambulance after being found unresponsive at home by his adult child. Last known well was approximately 18:00 yesterday. History of hypertension and atrial fibrillation, non-compliant with medications. On arrival, client was unresponsive to verbal stimuli, posturing to painful stimuli. Pupils were unequal, with the right pupil dilated and sluggishly reactive. Airway was compromised, and the client was intubated for protection. Non-contrast head CT revealed a large right-sided intraparenchymal hemorrhage with significant midline shift. Neurosurgery was consulted. Client transferred to ICU for continuous neurological monitoring and management of intracranial pressure. Family is at the bedside and has been updated on the client's critical condition.

S: Client remains intubated and sedated in the ICU for management of a large hemorrhagic stroke. B: Admitted at 03:00. CT showed a large right-sided hemorrhage with a 5mm midline shift. Mannitol 25g IV was administered at 04:00. Client has a history of hypertension and atrial fibrillation. Lives alone in a second-floor apartment, which may pose a barrier to discharge planning. A: Neurologically, GCS remains 6T. Right pupil remains dilated and sluggish. Vitals show a widening pulse pressure (BP 165/85, MAP 112) and bradycardia (HR 48), consistent with Cushing's triad. Temperature is trending up to 100.4°F (38.0°C). Serum sodium is now 148 mEq/L, and osmolality is elevated post-mannitol. ABG shows respiratory acidosis. R: Continue close neurological monitoring. Maintain head of bed at 30 degrees. Monitor serum osmolality and sodium closely. Awaiting neurosurgery for potential EVD placement for ICP monitoring and CSF drainage.

S: Good morning. This is the report on the 72-year-old male in ICU bed 4 with a large right-sided hemorrhagic stroke. B: He was intubated on admission at 03:00. CT showed a large bleed with midline shift. He received mannitol at 04:00. A: He remains sedated, GCS 6T. Vitals are showing Cushing's triad with a BP of 165/85 and heart rate of 48. R: Continue hourly neuro checks, maintain HOB at 30 degrees, and monitor for EVD placement by neurosurgery.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
Current Assessment (07:00)

General: Client remains intubated and sedated. No spontaneous movements noted.

Neurological: GCS 6T (E1, V1T, M4). Right pupil 5mm and sluggishly reactive to light, left pupil 3mm and reactive. Corneal reflex absent on the right. No response to sternal rub other than decorticate posturing.

Cardiovascular: Heart rate 48 bpm, regular. Blood pressure 165/85 mmHg. Pulses 2+ and equal bilaterally.

Respiratory: Ventilator settings: FiO2 40%, TV 500 mL, Rate 12. Breath sounds coarse bilaterally. ETCO2 within normal limits.

Gastrointestinal: Abdomen soft, non-distended. Bowel sounds hypoactive.

Genitourinary: Foley catheter in place, draining clear yellow urine.

Skin: Skin warm and dry. No areas of breakdown noted.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content:
CT Head without contrast (Today at 02:45)

Findings: Large intraparenchymal hematoma is identified within the right temporal and parietal lobes, measuring approximately 6 cm in diameter. There is significant surrounding vasogenic edema. Mass effect is noted with effacement of the adjacent sulci and compression of the right lateral ventricle. There is a 5 mm leftward subfalcine herniation (midline shift).

Impression: Acute large right-sided intraparenchymal hemorrhage with significant mass effect and early signs of herniation, consistent with a hemorrhagic stroke.

pedagogy.nclexCategory: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

pearls.title: Early Recognition of Cushing's Triad
pearls.content: Cushing's triad (hypertension with widening pulse pressure, bradycardia, and irregular respirations) is a late sign of increased ICP and requires immediate intervention.

pearls.title: Importance of Serum Osmolality Monitoring
pearls.content: When administering osmotic diuretics like mannitol, closely monitor serum osmolality to prevent dehydration and acute kidney injury. The target range is typically 300-320 mOsm/kg.

traps.title: Trap: Administering Hypotonic Fluids
traps.description: Hypotonic fluids can worsen cerebral edema by causing fluid to shift into brain cells, increasing ICP.
traps.strategy: Always use isotonic fluids (e.g., 0.9% normal saline) in patients with actual or potential increased ICP to avoid exacerbating cerebral edema.

traps.title: Trap: Positioning the Patient Supine
traps.description: Placing the patient in a supine position can impede venous outflow from the brain, increasing ICP.
traps.strategy: Maintain the head of the bed at 30 degrees and ensure neutral head alignment to promote venous drainage and reduce ICP.

mnemonics.title: ICP Management
mnemonics.description: Elevate HOB, Limit stimulation, Evaluate ABGs, Ventilate, Administer Mannitol, Titrate sedation, External Ventricular Drain (EVD)

mnemonics.title: Cushing's Triad
mnemonics.description: Blood Pressure (Hypertension with widening pulse pressure), Heart Rate (Bradycardia), Respiratory Rate (Irregular)
